title: Slow-cooker chicken cacciatore
subtitle: Hunter's chicken, slow-braised in tomato, olives and capers.
excerpt: Bone-in chicken thighs braised in a rich tomato sauce with olives, capers, white wine and herbs for six hours in the slow cooker. The sauce reduces and concentrates while the meat falls off the bone. Italian home cooking at its most unfussy. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Pollo alla cacciatora, the Italian hunter's chicken. The dish appears across central and northern Italian regional cooking; the southern Italian versions use olives and capers, the northern versions use mushrooms and wine. This recipe follows the Roman-influenced southern style. Ada Boni's Il Talismano della Felicità (1929) is the canonical mid-20th-century Italian source.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked chicken in the sauce for up to three months. Defrost overnight and reheat gently on the hob.
recipe.batchNotes: Makes excellent leftovers. The sauce deepens overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Excellent made the day before; reheat on the hob with the lid on over low heat for 20 minutes.

recipeTools.notes: For browning the chicken before slow cooking.

content.text: Cacciatore means hunter's style. The dish is a braise: chicken pieces cooked low and slow in tomato, wine and aromatics until the sauce reduces and the meat relaxes. In the slow cooker it needs no watching. Brown the chicken in a pan first, build the sauce in the same pan to capture the fond, and tip everything into the pot.

Bone-in thighs are better than breast here. They hold together through a long braise and contribute gelatin to the sauce as they cook; a boneless breast turns dry and stringy after six hours. The olives and capers go in at the start; they need the time to soften and integrate.

items.prepNote: one tin, crushed with your hand

content.text: Season {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Heat {{rapeseed-oil}} of oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Brown the thighs skin-side down for 4 to 5 minutes until the skin is golden and has released its fat. Turn and brown the other side for 2 minutes. Transfer to the slow-cooker pot.

Pour off excess fat from the pan, leaving about a tablespoon. Add {{onion}} sliced onion and {{pepper-red}} sliced pepper. Cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano; cook for 1 minute. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and let it bubble for 2 minutes, scraping the base of the pan. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{olives-black}} of olives, {{capers}} of capers and {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves. Stir and pour over the chicken in the slow cooker.
Cook on LOW for 6 to 7 hours or HIGH for 3 to 4 hours. The chicken is done when the meat pulls away easily from the bone. If you want a thicker sauce, remove the lid for the last 30 minutes on HIGH.

items.cause: Too much liquid, or lid trap released condensation.
items.fix: Remove the lid and cook on HIGH for 30 to 40 minutes. A slow cooker doesn't reduce the way a hob does; plan for this.

items.cause: Breast pieces used instead of thighs, or overcooked.
items.fix: Use bone-in thighs. Breast goes dry after 4 hours; check thighs at 6 rather than leaving for 8.

items.cause: Too many olives or capers, or tomatoes too acidic.
items.fix: Taste before serving; a pinch of sugar or a splash of cream calms an over-sharp sauce.

content.text: Cacciatore is a hunter's recipe in the sense that it uses what is at hand: a bird, some preserved vegetables, wine, herbs. It belongs on a weeknight when you want Italian flavour without standing over a pot. Serve with crusty bread to mop the sauce, or over polenta or pasta. It reheats better than it cooks fresh; leftovers are the point.
